title: Biblical History and Gospel Work in the Mediterranean
subtitle: Augustine School 2026 Sr. Trip
author: Jeff Johnson
body: Biblical History and Gospel Work in the Mediterranean

1. Rome, Italy
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Rome stands as perhaps the most significant city in the entire biblical narrative of the early church. The Apostle Paul addressed his most theologically comprehensive letter to the church at Rome (Romans 1:7), a congregation he had not yet visited but longed to see: "I long to see you, that I may impart to you some spiritual gift to strengthen you" (Romans 1:11). Paul's letter opens with a sweeping proclamation of the gospel as "the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek" (Romans 1:16).
Rome features prominently in the closing chapters of Acts. Paul arrived in Rome as a prisoner, yet even his imprisonment advanced the gospel (Philippians 1:12-13). Acts 28 describes how Paul was permitted to live in his own rented dwelling, where "he welcomed all who came to him, proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance" (Acts 28:30-31). These are the final words of Acts — a powerful conclusion testifying that no earthly power could silence the gospel.
Both the Apostle Paul and the Apostle Peter are historically associated with Rome. Paul wrote his prison epistles (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) from Rome, and tradition strongly holds that both Paul and Peter were martyred under Nero's persecution around AD 64-68. Paul was beheaded on the Ostian Way, while Peter was crucified upside down. Second Timothy — Paul's final letter — was written from Roman imprisonment with the solemn awareness that his "departure has come" (2 Timothy 4:6).
The church at Rome also features in Peter's first epistle; scholars widely identify "Babylon" in 1 Peter 5:13 as a coded reference to Rome, indicating Peter was writing from the city. Revelation's repeated use of "Babylon the great" (Revelation 17-18) carries strong allusions to Rome as the great oppressive empire of the apostolic age.
Gospel & Missions Work
Italy as a whole is one of the most significant evangelical mission fields in Western Europe. Despite its deep Christian heritage, less than 1% of Italy's population is evangelical Protestant, and the majority of Italians — including in Rome — have little to no exposure to a clear, personal presentation of the gospel of grace through faith in Christ alone.
One of the most important historic evangelical partners in Italian mission is the Waldensian Church — a Reformed body with roots stretching back to the 12th century, predating the Protestant Reformation by three hundred years. The Waldensians endured centuries of brutal persecution for their commitment to Scripture and the gospel, and they represent the oldest continuously existing Protestant witness on Italian soil. Today the Waldensian Church, formally part of the World Communion of Reformed Churches, maintains congregations throughout Italy including in Rome, and has historically been a key partner for Presbyterian mission engagement in the country. Both the PCUSA and Mission to the World (MTW, PCA) have worked in partnership with Waldensian and Italian evangelical institutions.
The Italian Baptist Union (Unione Cristiana Evangelica Battista d'Italia, UCEBI) is another significant evangelical body with a long history of gospel witness in Rome and throughout Italy. Italian Baptist congregations have been active in church planting and evangelism across the country, and the International Mission Board (SBC) has maintained partnerships with Italian Baptist churches and supported new church-planting efforts in Rome and surrounding regions. Greater Europe Mission and other evangelical agencies work alongside these Baptist and Reformed efforts. Campus ministry organizations including IFES and The Navigators maintain work among Rome's large student population, providing gospel witness in the intellectual heart of the city where Paul once proclaimed Christ "with all boldness and without hindrance."

2. Naples, Italy
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
While Naples itself is not mentioned by name in Scripture, the region directly figures into Paul's final journey to Rome. Acts 28:13 records that Paul's ship sailed from Rhegium to Puteoli — the ancient Roman port city that is modern-day Pozzuoli, located in the Bay of Naples just west of Naples proper. It was the principal harbor for grain ships from Alexandria, and it was here that Paul disembarked for the final overland leg to Rome.
Remarkably, Acts 28:14 records that Paul "found brothers there and was invited to stay with them for seven days." This brief note reveals that an established Christian community already existed in the Bay of Naples region by the time Paul arrived — likely carried there by the constant maritime traffic between Rome, Alexandria, and the eastern Mediterranean. The gospel had preceded the Apostle Paul to this region. The Spirit of God does not wait for apostles to move first; He goes ahead.
The area around Naples is also historically connected to the Sibylline Oracles and the cave of the Cumaean Sibyl at Cumae, just north of Puteoli — a site of ancient prophecy and pagan religion deeply familiar to the first-century world Paul was evangelizing.
Gospel & Missions Work
Naples is often cited as one of the most spiritually needy cities in Western Europe. Deep poverty, generations of social dysfunction tied to organized crime (the Camorra), and a pervasive sense of fatalism have shaped a population that is both hungry for hope and resistant to change. The gospel of grace — that a person can be fully forgiven, genuinely transformed, and given a living hope through faith in Christ — speaks directly into this context.
The Waldensian Church, Italy's historic Reformed body, has maintained a presence in the Naples region and throughout southern Italy for generations, representing the longest-standing evangelical witness in a region dominated by Roman Catholicism. Presbyterian mission engagement through PCUSA and MTW (PCA) has supported Waldensian and Italian evangelical institutions that serve this region.
Italian Baptist churches have also been active in the Naples area, with the Italian Baptist Union maintaining congregations and church-planting efforts in Campania. The International Mission Board (SBC) has partnered with Italian Baptist work in southern Italy, and Operation Mobilization has maintained long-term presence in Naples through literature distribution and evangelism. Several evangelical churches in the city have developed significant social ministries — working with drug addicts, the homeless, and families trapped in cycles of poverty — as tangible expressions of the gospel's transforming power in one of Europe's most challenged urban environments.

3. Crete (Chania), Greece
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Crete appears at several pivotal moments in the New Testament. Most dramatically, Acts 27 records Paul's fateful voyage past the island. Sailing under Roman guard toward Rome, the ship passed along the southern coast of Crete, putting in at Fair Havens near the city of Lasea. Paul warned the centurion against continuing the voyage: "I can see that this voyage will be with disaster and great loss, not only of the cargo and the ship, but also of our lives" (Acts 27:10). His warning was ignored, the ship was caught by the violent Euroclydon (northeastern gale), and after 14 days adrift, they were shipwrecked at Malta. The episode powerfully illustrates both Paul's spiritual discernment and the sovereign providence of God in preserving his servant for the mission ahead.
Crete is also directly addressed in the New Testament epistles. Paul left his trusted coworker Titus on the island with specific instructions: "This is why I left you in Crete, so that you might put what remained into order, and appoint elders in every town as I directed you" (Titus 1:5). The letter to Titus provides some of the most practical instruction in all of Paul's writings on church governance, elder qualifications, and sound doctrine.
Paul's frank quotation of the Cretan poet Epimenides — "Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons" (Titus 1:12) — reflects both the cultural reputation of the island and the transforming power of the gospel: Christ saves people out of exactly these patterns and forms communities of truth, self-control, and diligence where none existed before. Cretans were also present in Jerusalem on the Day of Pentecost (Acts 2:11), meaning converts from Crete were among the very first recipients of the Holy Spirit, likely carrying the gospel home before Paul ever arrived.
Gospel & Missions Work
Crete presents a challenging but not unreachable evangelical mission field. The island's population is deeply shaped by Greek Orthodox identity, where religious affiliation is inseparable from ethnic and national belonging. To embrace evangelical Christianity in this context can mean navigating significant social and family pressure, yet genuine conversions do occur, and small evangelical congregations exist in Heraklion and other Cretan cities.
Presbyterian and Reformed mission engagement with Greece has a long and significant history. The Greek Evangelical Church — a Reformed denomination with Presbyterian polity — was founded in the 19th century largely through the missionary work of Dr. Jonas King and the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM), in which Presbyterian participation was substantial. The Greek Evangelical Church maintains congregations on Crete and throughout Greece, representing the direct fruit of that early Reformed missionary investment. Both MTW (PCA) and the PCUSA have supported the Greek Evangelical Church as a key partner in ongoing Greek ministry.
Baptist mission has also contributed to evangelical witness in Greece. The International Mission Board (SBC) has supported church-planting efforts in Greece including Crete, and Greek Baptist congregations form part of the small but committed evangelical community on the island. Greek-language media ministry — through Trans World Radio and other organizations — plays a crucial role across the Greek islands, where communities are too small or too resistant for a resident missionary presence, carrying the gospel of grace into homes across the island that Titus was commissioned to evangelize nearly two thousand years ago.

4. Ephesus (Turkey)
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Ephesus is arguably the most extensively documented city in the entire New Testament outside of Jerusalem. It served as the hub of Paul's third missionary journey, where he spent approximately three years — longer than anywhere else recorded in Acts. Acts 19 provides a vivid account of that ministry: the disciples of John who received the Holy Spirit (Acts 19:1-7), Paul's two-year teaching ministry in the hall of Tyrannus where "all the residents of Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks" (Acts 19:10), the extraordinary miracles, the burning of occult books valued at 50,000 pieces of silver (Acts 19:19), and the riot of the silversmiths stirred up by Demetrius in defense of Artemis (Acts 19:23-41). The temple of Artemis was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, and Paul's gospel directly threatened the idol-making economy — a reminder that the gospel always has economic implications when it confronts false religion.
Paul's letter to the Ephesians, written from prison, sets forth one of the most exalted theological visions in all of Scripture: the eternal purpose of God to unite all things in Christ (Ephesians 1:10), the church as Christ's body and fullness (Ephesians 1:22-23), salvation by grace through faith "not of works, so that no one may boast" (Ephesians 2:8-9), and the full armor of God for spiritual warfare (Ephesians 6:10-18).
Paul's farewell address to the Ephesian elders at Miletus (Acts 20:17-38) is among the most moving passages in Acts — "I did not shrink from declaring to you anything that was profitable" (Acts 20:20) — and his warning about "fierce wolves" arising from within the church (Acts 20:29-30) proved tragically prescient. Ephesus is also one of the Seven Churches of Revelation (Revelation 2:1-7), commended for doctrinal discernment but rebuked for having abandoned its first love. The risen Christ's warning that He would remove their lampstand unless they repented is a solemn reminder to every generation that the gospel must be held fast, not merely inherited.
Gospel & Missions Work
Ephesus today lies in Turkey, a nation that is approximately 99% Muslim. The ruins near the town of Selçuk draw millions of tourists annually, creating a remarkable open-air context for reflection on the history of the gospel and for personal witness to fellow visitors from around the world.
Presbyterian mission has one of the deepest historical roots in this region of any Protestant tradition. The American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM), with strong Presbyterian participation, and later the explicitly Presbyterian Board of Foreign Missions, sent missionaries to Anatolia beginning in the early 19th century. These missionaries established schools, hospitals, and churches throughout the region, translated the Bible into Turkish and Armenian, and saw significant fruit among Armenian and Greek Christian communities. This remarkable investment came to a tragic end with the Armenian Genocide (1915-1923) and subsequent population exchanges, which effectively ended organized Christian communities across Anatolia. The PCUSA maintained mission engagement in Turkey for well over a century, and MTW (PCA) continues to support the small Turkish Protestant community today.
Baptist mission has likewise engaged Turkey. The International Mission Board (SBC) has supported church-planting among the Turkish people, one of the largest unreached people groups in the world. Together, Baptist and Presbyterian mission investment represents a sustained, if costly, commitment to the region where Paul once declared that all of Asia heard the word of the Lord — and where that word urgently needs to be heard again.

5. Mykonos, Greece
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Mykonos does not appear by name in Scripture, but it lies in the heart of the Aegean Sea through which Paul repeatedly sailed during his missionary journeys. The island sits just a few miles northeast of Delos — one of the most important religious centers of the ancient Greek world, considered the birthplace of Apollo and Artemis in Greek mythology, its massive sanctuary complex drawing pilgrims from across the Mediterranean.
Paul's missionary travels took him repeatedly through these waters. Acts 20:15 records that on his final journey to Jerusalem, Paul's vessel passed "opposite Chios" and then "touched at Samos" — a route passing directly through the waters surrounding Mykonos and Delos. The proximity of Delos, with its dense concentration of idol temples and pagan worship, to the sea lanes Paul traveled is a reminder that the gospel moved through a world saturated with false religion — not unlike our own.
The commercial sea lanes through the Cyclades connected Corinth, Ephesus, Alexandria, and Rome — the arteries of empire along which both commerce and the gospel of Christ traveled. Every island in this chain was part of the world Christ was reclaiming through the proclamation of His apostles.
Gospel & Missions Work
Mykonos is one of the world's most internationally recognized tourist destinations, drawing millions of visitors annually from across Europe, America, and beyond. This extraordinary concentration of people from virtually every nation creates a unique mission opportunity: the nations, in a sense, come to this small island.
Established evangelical Christianity is very limited among the island's permanent population, as is true across the Greek Cyclades. The Greek Evangelical Church — the Reformed denomination with Presbyterian polity — represents the primary historic Protestant network in the Greek island world, and Baptist mission through the IMB has contributed to broader Greek evangelical infrastructure. Both traditions support Greek-language Christian media, literature, and ministry networks that reach island communities like Mykonos where no resident congregation exists.
For the Baptist or Presbyterian visitor to Mykonos, the opportunity is primarily one of personal witness — conversations with locals and fellow travelers that the extraordinary setting naturally invites. The island's beauty is itself a natural opening for conversations about the Creator and about the Christ who is Lord of every sea and every shore. Every believer visiting Mykonos carries the same commission Paul carried past these waters: to bear witness to the One in whom we live and move and have our being.

6. Athens, Greece
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Athens occupies a uniquely prominent place in Paul's missionary narrative as the intellectual and cultural capital of the ancient world. Acts 17:16-34 provides one of the most celebrated accounts of gospel proclamation in all of Scripture. Arriving in Athens while waiting for Silas and Timothy, Paul's "spirit was provoked within him as he saw that the city was full of idols" (Acts 17:16). He reasoned in the synagogue and in the marketplace daily, drawing the attention of Epicurean and Stoic philosophers who brought him before the Areopagus — the ancient council that met on Mars Hill.
Paul's address on the Areopagus is a masterpiece of contextual apologetics. He begins with careful observation: "Men of Athens, I perceive that in every way you are very religious. For as I passed along and observed the objects of your worship, I found also an altar with this inscription: 'To the unknown god'" (Acts 17:22-23). He then moves from their own poetic traditions ("In him we live and move and have our being" and "For we are indeed his offspring" — both from Greek poets, Acts 17:28) to the proclamation of the one Creator God, the appointed day of judgment, and the resurrection of Jesus.
Paul's method is instructive: he found genuine points of contact in the culture without compromising the gospel's distinctive claims. He did not soften the offense of the resurrection — the very point at which some in his audience mocked — because it was non-negotiable. Real conversions followed, including Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris (Acts 17:34). The gospel divides and saves even in the most sophisticated intellectual settings.
Gospel & Missions Work
Athens is one of the most active evangelical mission fields in Greece, and both Baptist and Presbyterian traditions have contributed significantly to gospel work in the city. The defining challenge is that the vast majority of Athenians who consider themselves Christian have never heard a clear, personal presentation of justification by faith alone — Christianity functions as a cultural inheritance rather than a personal encounter with Christ through the gospel.
The Greek Evangelical Church — founded through the ABCFM and Presbyterian mission investment in the 19th century — has its headquarters in Athens and maintains several congregations in the city. Dr. Jonas King's early ministry, the establishment of evangelical educational institutions, and decades of literature and Bible distribution built a foundation that continues to bear fruit. MTW (PCA) and the PCUSA support Greek Evangelical Church partnerships as part of their ongoing Athens engagement.
The International Mission Board (SBC) has also maintained Baptist church-planting work in Athens, and Greek Baptist congregations are part of the city's small but growing evangelical community. Hellenic Ministries, The Navigators, and IFES (among university students) work alongside these Baptist and Presbyterian efforts. Perhaps the most remarkable recent development is the growth of gospel witness among Iranian, Afghan, and Syrian refugees — many proving extraordinarily receptive to the gospel — creating thriving new congregations that are reshaping the evangelical landscape of Athens. Paul stood on Mars Hill and declared the unknown God. That same proclamation, carried by Baptist and Reformed missionaries and their Greek partners, is now reaching people from across the world who have arrived at Athens' shores.

7. Santorini, Greece
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Santorini — known in antiquity as Thera — does not appear by name in the biblical text, yet it lay squarely within the Aegean maritime world of the New Testament church. The island sits approximately 120 miles southeast of Athens and was a known waypoint in ancient Mediterranean navigation. Its dramatic caldera — the remnant of one of the most catastrophic volcanic eruptions in human history (c. 1600 BC, the Minoan eruption) — would have been a well-known geographical feature to ancient sailors.
Thera lay along the commercial and naval routes connecting mainland Greece to Crete, Rhodes, and the eastern Mediterranean — routes traversed repeatedly by Paul and other early missionaries. The broader context of Paul's Aegean ministry (Acts 16-20), his letters to the Corinthians addressing believers within sailing distance of these islands, and the seven churches of Revelation (Revelation 2-3) all situate the early church within a world where these waters were regularly traveled by those bearing the gospel.
The sea itself — often violent and unpredictable, as Paul knew firsthand from his shipwreck off Malta and his multiple open-ocean journeys — was both obstacle and highway in the spread of the kingdom of God. The apostolic mission pressed through every natural barrier, and no island in the Aegean lay beyond the reach of the gospel's advance.
Gospel & Missions Work
Santorini today is one of the world's most iconic tourist destinations, drawing well over two million visitors annually. Its famous white-washed villages perched above the caldera attract people from virtually every nation on earth, creating one of the most internationally diverse mission contexts imaginable — compressed onto a small volcanic island in the Aegean Sea.
The permanent population of Santorini is small (roughly 15,000 residents), and established evangelical Christianity is essentially absent as a local community. Presbyterian mission investment in the Greek Evangelical Church and Baptist mission through the IMB have both contributed to broader Greek evangelical infrastructure — including Greek-language Bible translation, Christian literature, and media ministry — that reaches island communities like Santorini even in the absence of a resident congregation or missionary.
For the Baptist or Presbyterian visitor to Santorini, the primary opportunity is personal witness — conversations with locals and fellow travelers that the extraordinary setting of the island naturally invites. The created beauty of Santorini — the caldera, the light, the sea — is itself a powerful witness to the Creator and a natural opening for gospel conversation. The whole of the Aegean island world remains a mission field that Baptist and Reformed churches, through partnerships with Greek evangelical denominations and support for media ministry, continue to engage even where direct presence is not yet possible.

8. Bar, Montenegro
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Bar (ancient Antibari) lies on the eastern Adriatic coast of modern Montenegro, situated in the ancient region of Illyricum. While Bar itself does not appear in Scripture, its region is directly referenced by the Apostle Paul in one of the most remarkable summary statements of his missionary career. In Romans 15:19, Paul writes: "from Jerusalem and all the way around to Illyricum I have fulfilled the ministry of the gospel of Christ." This sweeping geographical statement — covering from Jerusalem westward all the way to the Adriatic coast — marks Illyricum as the northwestern boundary of Paul's completed apostolic theater.
Whether Paul actually entered Illyricum or approached its borders during his extended time in Macedonia (Acts 20:3) has been debated by scholars. Either way, the Adriatic coast near modern Montenegro stood at the very edge of Paul's apostolic vision and labors. The region also appears in Paul's final correspondence: "Titus [has gone] to Dalmatia" (2 Timothy 4:10) — the southern Adriatic coast that includes the region near Bar — indicating that active apostolic mission work was being carried out on this coastline during Paul's final years of ministry.
Paul's stated theological drive was always to press toward regions where Christ had not been named, "so that I would not build on someone else's foundation" (Romans 15:20). The Illyrian coast represented exactly this kind of frontier — and Paul was constitutionally unable to leave unreached people beyond his reach without sending workers.
Gospel & Missions Work
Montenegro today represents one of the smallest and least-reached evangelical mission fields in Europe. The country's population of roughly 620,000 is predominantly Eastern Orthodox, with a significant Muslim minority, and in both communities religious identity functions more as ethnic and cultural belonging than as personal, saving faith. The biblical gospel of grace through faith in Christ alone is largely unknown in the majority of the population.
Presbyterian mission engagement in the western Balkans has been part of broader Reformed mission strategy for Eastern Europe. The PCUSA maintained mission presence across the former Yugoslavia through much of the 20th century, and MTW (PCA) has supported church-planting efforts in the Balkan region. The Reformed Church in Serbia — a small but historically significant denomination — represents a Reformed presence in the broader neighborhood, and Presbyterian mission bodies have worked in partnership with Reformed churches across the Balkans to support evangelism and church planting.
The International Mission Board (SBC) has also engaged the western Balkans through Baptist church-planting partnerships. A handful of evangelical congregations in Podgorica and other Montenegrin cities — supported by both Reformed and Baptist mission investment alongside organizations like YWAM and Agape (CCC) — represent the current evangelical witness in this profoundly underserved country. Bar and the Montenegrin coastline remain a place where the gospel needs to be carried afresh, and where the combined investment of Baptist and Presbyterian mission is part of honoring the apostolic mandate to go where Christ has not yet been named.

9. Corfu, Greece
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Corfu (ancient Corcyra) is the largest of the Ionian Islands, located just off the northwestern coast of Greece near the Albanian border. It sits on the maritime route between the eastern Mediterranean and the Adriatic Sea — a critically important passage for ancient ships traveling between the Aegean and Italy, and therefore directly on the sea lanes Paul would have used in his journeys to and from Rome.
While Corcyra is not mentioned by name in the New Testament, Paul's voyage to Rome (Acts 27-28) and his likely later travels referenced in the pastoral epistles would have brought his vessel through or near Corcyrean waters. The island was the "gateway to the Adriatic" in ancient navigation, well-known and frequently called upon by vessels on the Italy-to-Greece run.
Paul's journey to Rome, his subsequent release and further travels, and the extension of apostolic mission toward Illyricum and Dalmatia (Romans 15:19; 2 Timothy 4:10) all placed Corfu squarely within the geographical corridor of the early church's westward gospel advance. The Ionian Islands were part of the same Greek-speaking world Paul addressed throughout his ministry, and the gospel that traveled the Adriatic sea lanes would have touched this island in the apostolic generation.
Gospel & Missions Work
Corfu has a small but notable evangelical history, partly rooted in the period of British protectorate rule (1814-1864), when Anglican worship was formally established on the island. This period created some openness to Protestant Christianity more broadly, and the Ionian Islands were among the first parts of Greece to have sustained Protestant missionary contact.
The Greek Evangelical Church — the Reformed denomination with Presbyterian polity — has had a presence in the Ionian Islands as part of its broader national network, representing the fruit of 19th-century Presbyterian and ABCFM missionary investment. MTW (PCA) and the PCUSA support this denomination's ongoing work in the region. Baptist mission through the IMB has also contributed to broader Greek evangelical networks that provide resources and partnership to smaller island communities.
Albania, just across the narrow channel from Corfu, is one of the most remarkable evangelical mission stories in Europe. After decades of enforced state atheism, Albania has seen extraordinary evangelical church growth since 1991, with both Baptist and Presbyterian/Reformed mission bodies — including the IMB and MTW — playing significant roles in that work. Southern Baptist missionaries were among the first to enter Albania after the fall of communism, establishing Baptist churches that grew rapidly. The proximity of Corfu to this flourishing Albanian evangelical church creates natural opportunities for cross-border fellowship and collaborative mission — a modern echo of the apostolic corridor that connected these shores in the first century.

10. Sicily (Messina), Italy
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Sicily appears directly in the narrative of Paul's journey to Rome. Acts 28:12 records: "And landing at Syracuse, we stayed there for three days." Syracuse — on the southeastern coast of Sicily — was the largest and most important city of ancient Sicily, and Paul's three-day stop there is explicitly recorded by Luke. Though the passage gives no details of evangelistic activity, Paul's consistent pattern throughout Acts was to proclaim the gospel wherever he found himself, and Syracuse had a significant Jewish community — one of the oldest in the western Mediterranean — which would have been his natural starting point.
From Syracuse, Acts 28:13 records that the ship "made a circuit and arrived at Rhegium" — the toe of the Italian boot directly across the narrow Strait of Messina from Sicily. Messina sits at the very northern tip of Sicily on this strait, making it the point geographically closest to Paul's passage. The Strait of Messina — barely two miles wide at its narrowest — was one of the most strategically important and navigationally treacherous passages in the ancient Mediterranean, associated in Greek mythology with Scylla and Charybdis.
The whole of Sicily was thoroughly integrated into the Roman imperial world Paul was evangelizing, and the island's position at the crossroads of Mediterranean trade routes meant the gospel would have reached its shores quickly through the ordinary movement of commerce and people — as it had already reached the Bay of Naples before Paul arrived (Acts 28:14).
Gospel & Missions Work
Sicily presents a genuinely active and growing evangelical mission field within the broader context of southern Italy. Baptist and Reformed traditions have both contributed meaningfully to evangelical witness on the island, and their combined presence represents a significant portion of Sicily's small but growing evangelical community.
The Waldensian Church — Italy's historic Reformed denomination — has maintained a presence in Sicily as part of its national network, and the PCUSA's historic relationship with the Waldensians includes support for their Sicilian congregations and social ministries. MTW (PCA) has also supported Italian evangelical church-planting in the south through Reformed partnerships. The Italian Baptist Union has likewise maintained congregations and church-planting efforts across Sicily, and the International Mission Board (SBC) has partnered with Italian Baptist work in the region. These Baptist and Reformed congregations together form the core of established evangelical Christianity in Messina and other Sicilian cities.
The island's history of deep poverty and social hardship has created a population genuinely open to the hope the gospel provides. Evangelical churches in Sicily — Baptist, Reformed, and Brethren — often function as genuine communities of grace where people from broken backgrounds encounter forgiveness, belonging, and transformed lives. African-led evangelical congregations have also become significant witnesses on the island, many arriving as migrants and bringing with them a vibrant faith that is making a tangible impact alongside the historic Baptist and Reformed witness in the place where Paul first stepped ashore en route to Rome.

11. Barcelona, Spain
Biblical & Historical Scriptural Mentions
Barcelona and the broader region of Hispania (Spain) hold a unique place in Pauline missiology as the stated ultimate destination of his westward apostolic ambition. In Romans 15:24, Paul writes: "I hope to see you in passing as I go to Spain, and to be helped on my journey there by you, once I have enjoyed your company for a while." He repeats this intention a few verses later: "I will leave for Spain by way of you" (Romans 15:28). Spain represented the western edge of the known Roman world — the Pillars of Hercules (modern Gibraltar) marked the boundary of the Mediterranean — and Paul's burning desire to preach "not where Christ has already been named, so that I would not build on someone else's foundation" (Romans 15:20) drove him to envision mission to the uttermost geographical reaches of the empire.
Whether Paul ever reached Spain is one of the most discussed questions in New Testament scholarship. Acts ends with Paul's first Roman imprisonment, and the pastoral epistles imply a later release and further travels. Clement of Rome, writing around AD 96, states that Paul "reached the farthest bounds of the West" — widely interpreted as Spain. The Muratorian Fragment (c. AD 170) explicitly states "the journey of Paul from the city [Rome] to Spain." The weight of early testimony favors Paul having fulfilled this ambition, making Barcelona (ancient Barcino) — as a significant Roman colonial city on the northeastern Mediterranean coast — a plausible destination in the apostolic mission to Hispania.
Paul's Spain ambition reflects a missionary theology in which no people, no city, no region is beyond the reach of the gospel — and in which the church is obligated to press toward the unreached. This is the theology of Romans 10:14-15: "How are they to hear without someone preaching? And how are they to preach unless they are sent?"
Gospel & Missions Work
Spain is one of the most encouraging evangelical mission stories in contemporary Europe, and both Baptist and Presbyterian traditions have been meaningfully part of that story. For centuries, evangelical Christianity was violently suppressed in Spain, and Protestant believers who attempted to bring the biblical gospel to the Iberian Peninsula paid with their lives under the Inquisition. Religious freedom only arrived with the restoration of democracy after 1975, and in the decades since, evangelical Christianity has grown remarkably.
The Spanish Evangelical Church (Iglesia Evangélica Española, IEE) is a Reformed denomination with Presbyterian roots, formed through the merger of several Protestant bodies after religious freedom was restored. The PCUSA has maintained a historic relationship with the IEE, and MTW (PCA) has supported church-planting in Spain including in Barcelona and Catalonia, working alongside Spanish evangelical partners with Reformed theological commitments. The Spanish Baptist Union (Unión Evangélica Bautista Española, UEBE) is one of Spain's most active evangelical denominations, with a significant presence in Barcelona and throughout Catalonia. The International Mission Board (SBC) has long partnered with Spanish Baptists in church-planting and evangelism, and this partnership has been one of the most fruitful evangelical mission relationships in Spanish history. Baptist and Reformed congregations together form a substantial part of Barcelona's growing evangelical community.
Latin American evangelical immigrants — many from Baptist and Reformed church backgrounds in Colombia, Venezuela, Mexico, and beyond — have been a remarkable catalyst for evangelical growth in Barcelona, reversing the direction of mission: the fruit of centuries of gospel work in the Americas is now returning to evangelize the mother country. Paul longed to go to Spain. That longing is being fulfilled today in Barcelona, through the combined witness of Baptist and Presbyterian missionaries and their Spanish and Latin American partners.

title: Grace in the Furnace
subtitle: How suffering shapes us into vessels of Christ
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: The potter does not despise the cracked clay. He sees in every fracture an opportunity — a place where his hands can press deeper, where the fire can do its refining work. Biblical counseling begins here: not with solutions, but with a theology of the kiln.
body: The potter does not despise the cracked clay. He sees in every fracture an opportunity — a place where his hands can press deeper, where the fire can do its refining work. Biblical counseling begins here: not with solutions, but with a theology of the kiln.

Romans 8:28 is often quoted as a comfort verse, deployed quickly in the presence of grief as though the pain were an embarrassment to be expedited. But Paul's argument is richer and more demanding. He does not promise that all things are good; he promises that God works all things — including the broken, the bitter, the bewildering — together for good to those who are called according to his purpose.

That 'purpose' (prothesis) is not vague. Paul defines it immediately: to be conformed to the image of his Son. Conformity to Christ is the furnace's aim. The counselor's task, then, is not to remove the heat but to help the sufferer understand what the heat is for.

This does not minimize pain. It dignifies it. When we tell a grieving mother that her loss is purposeless randomness, we offer her nothing but a cold universe. When we tell her it is being shaped by a Father who gave his own Son — who did not spare him — we give her something to stand on even in the ash.

title: Social Media and the Distracted Soul
subtitle: Reclaiming attention as an act of worship
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Attention is not a neutral faculty. Scripture treats it as a worshipful act. What we behold, we become — and the architecture of social media is designed to fragment beholding into an infinite scroll of competing claimants for the soul.
body: Attention is not a neutral faculty. Scripture treats it as a worshipful act. What we behold, we become — and the architecture of social media is designed to fragment beholding into an infinite scroll of competing claimants for the soul.

Psalm 115 draws the oldest connection between idolatry and transformation: those who make idols become like them. The idol of the social feed is not stone but pixel — yet the dynamic is identical. It promises significance (likes), belonging (followers), and identity (personal brand). These are not trivial desires. They are the desires that only Christ can fill.

Biblical counseling in this space must resist two failures: the moralistic ('just stop using it') and the therapeutic ('set healthy screen limits'). Both treat the behavior without the heart. The deeper question is always: what does this person believe social media can give them that they cannot find in Christ?

The goal is not Luddism. It is liturgical reordering — helping the counselee rebuild a life of attention whose center of gravity is Word, prayer, and embodied community, rather than the dopamine pulse of notification.

title: Come to Me, All Who Labor
subtitle: The yoke of Christ as rest, not burden
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: The invitation of Matthew 11:28 is the strangest paradox in the Gospels. Jesus calls the weary to take on a yoke. Rest is found not in the removal of burden but in the exchange of burdens — from the crushing weight of self-justification to the gentle load of grace.
body: The invitation of Matthew 11:28 is the strangest paradox in the Gospels. Jesus calls the weary to take on a yoke. Rest is found not in the removal of burden but in the exchange of burdens — from the crushing weight of self-justification to the gentle load of grace.

First-century listeners heard 'yoke' as a rabbinic metaphor for Torah observance and discipleship. Every teacher had a yoke — his interpretive school, his demands, his system. Jesus is not abolishing yokes; he is contrasting his yoke with every other. 'I am gentle and lowly in heart,' he says. No rabbi before him ever described himself that way.

The weariness Jesus addresses is not primarily physical. It is the exhaustion of performing for an audience that is never satisfied — whether that audience is family, culture, social media, or the demanding tribunal of the self. The yoke of Christ is a transfer of jurisdiction: from the court of human opinion to the verdict already spoken at the cross.

In counseling, we meet this weariness constantly. The anxious man who cannot rest because he cannot prove his worth. The perfectionistic woman who serves God as though grace had a co-pay. The gospel addresses not their behavior but their court. Christ has already spoken the verdict: Beloved.

title: The Counselor as Barnabas
subtitle: Encouragement as a theological act
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Barnabas means 'son of encouragement' — yet that title was given, not born. His name was Joseph. The church renamed him for what he did, and what he did was see potential in people others had written off. This is the counselor's vocation.
body: Barnabas means 'son of encouragement' — yet that title was given, not born. His name was Joseph. The church renamed him for what he did, and what he did was see potential in people others had written off. This is the counselor's vocation.

The Greek word paraklesis — encouragement, exhortation, comfort — shares its root with parakletos, the word Jesus uses for the Holy Spirit. This is not accidental. Encouragement, at its depth, is the Spirit's work expressed through human presence. The counselor does not manufacture hope; he mediates it.

Barnabas appears at two critical junctures: he vouches for the feared Saul to a suspicious Jerusalem church, and he later refuses to abandon John Mark when Paul would. In both cases, he extends trust to the person others found disqualifying. This is not naivety. It is a theology of second chances rooted in the God who restored Peter.

Good biblical counseling looks for what God is still doing in a person, not merely cataloging what has broken. It asks: what does redemption look like here? Not what does management look like. Not what does compliance look like. What does resurrection look like, in this particular life, at this particular moment?

title: Anxiety and the Sovereignty of God
subtitle: Why Philippians 4:6 is not a command to simply calm down
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Philippians 4:6 is one of the most quoted and most misapplied verses in pastoral ministry. 'Do not be anxious about anything' is read as a command to stop feeling anxious — as though anxiety were simply a moral failure correctable by willpower. Paul's meaning is richer and more merciful than that.
body: Philippians 4:6 is one of the most quoted and most misapplied verses in pastoral ministry. 'Do not be anxious about anything' is read as a command to stop feeling anxious — as though anxiety were simply a moral failure correctable by willpower. Paul's meaning is richer and more merciful than that.

The word translated 'be anxious' (merimnao) carries the sense of a divided mind — a heart pulled in multiple directions by competing concerns. Paul is not condemning the anxious person; he is diagnosing a condition and prescribing a reorientation. 'In everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God.'

Notice the structure: the antidote to divided attention is not focused attention on one's own feelings but directed speech toward God. The anxious person is not told to think less about their problem; they are told to bring the problem to the One who holds all things. Prayer is the transfer of burden.

This is why the passage ends not with 'your anxiety will disappear' but with 'the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds.' The peace comes after the prayer. And it is a peace that outpaces comprehension — meaning the circumstances may not change, but the orientation of the heart does.
